system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 12: Dominant 7th Chords and the Cycle

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 12 — Dominant 7th Chords and the Cycle

_Pages 65-68._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Augmented symmetry parallels diminished

> Augmented chords repeat themselves every 4 frets the same way diminished chords repeat
> every 3 frets

**Significance (p. 63).** Establishes the augmented chord's 4-fret symmetry analogous to diminished's 3-fret.

## Augmented as 7+ no b7

> It is wise to think of the + chord
> as a 7+ with no 57

**Significance (p. 63).** Reframes augmented as dominant-derived (7#5 minus b7) for functional use.

## Dominant 3-fret motion rule

> Almost any dominant type chord can be moved in intervals of 3 frets as long as the LAST chord
> resolves into wherever you are going next.

**Significance (p. 63).** Defines the diminished-cycle dominant motion technique.

## Altered dominant 2-fret motion

> Dominant chords that have Sths and/or #5ths may be used in 2 FRET INTERVALS effectively,
> still being careful to resolve nicely.

**Significance (p. 63).** Defines whole-tone parallel dominant motion for altered chords.

## Minor-major-7 for dominant a half-step lower

> m# type chords can be used in place of dominant chords whose roots are 1 fret lower (like
> Cm#/9 for B7). This will only work if the dominant chord is being used as a V7 or V7 function.

**Significance (p. 64).** Specific substitution rule connecting m(maj7) chords to dominants.

## 11b9 used only for V7

> The 1159 chords are usually used for the V7 chord of a key, but not often for others

**Significance (p. 64).** Restricts 11b9 to authentic-dominant function.

## Cycle of 4ths/5ths foundation

> In most popular songs, chords of the dominant family are used in certain common patterns. One
> key for understanding these patterns is found in what is known as the cycle of 4ths (also called the
> cycle of 5ths).

**Significance (p. 65).** Introduces the cycle as the organizing structure of common-practice progressions.

## Cycle-rooted chords in series

> CHORDS WITH ROOTS
> BASED ON THE CYCLE OF

**Significance (p. 65).** Names the cycle-based-series concept central to the chapter.

## Modulating defined

> changing to new keys is called MODULATING.

**Significance (p. 65).** Defines modulation terminology.

## ii-V smooths modulation

> a smooth transition can be achieved if the new key is
> preceded by a IIm7 V7 progression OF THAT NEW KEY.

**Significance (p. 65).** Core modulation technique: precede new tonic with its ii-V.

## V7 function defined

> When dominant chords are used as the V7 of the key or THE V7 of a V7, this will be called using
>
> the chord as a V7FUNCTION OR DOMINANT FUNCTION.

**Significance (p. 67).** Defines the secondary-dominant concept underlying cycle progressions.

## Common tones smooth modulations

> COMMON TONES“on the top notes of the chords will
>
> sometimes help smooth out the transitions.

**Significance (p. 67).** Voice-leading aid: shared top notes ease key transitions.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
